questions.prompt: Standard pattern for a styled component that accepts a className prop?

choices.text: Use the `cn` helper (`cn = (...args) => twMerge(clsx(args))`). The component composes internal classes with the user's className via cn(): `cn('px-4 py-2 bg-blue-600', className)`. `clsx` handles conditionals; `twMerge` resolves conflicting utilities so the user's className overrides predictably (their `px-8` wins over internal `px-4`). Also extend native HTML props (`React.ButtonHTMLAttributes<HTMLButtonElement>`) and spread `...props` onto the element — consumers can pass any standard attribute (onClick, disabled, aria-*) without enumerating them. Every shadcn/ui component follows this pattern; it's the 2026 standard for component libraries.

questions.explanation: cn() = twMerge(clsx(...)) for class composition + spread native props for full attribute support.

questions.prompt: Button has 12 props: variant, size, icon, iconPosition, loading, loadingText, href, fullWidth, iconColor, asLink, disabled, autoFocus. Better approach?

choices.text: Composition. Keep core variants (variant, size, disabled) as props; let consumers compose for the rest: `<Button asChild><Link href='...'><IconSave />Save</Link></Button>`. Icons become children — no iconPosition prop, just JSX order. Link behavior via asChild (Radix pattern) or `as` prop. Loading state via a `<Spinner />` rendered as children or a `<LoadingButton>` variant. Mega-buttons collapse under prop growth; composition scales. The shadcn/ui approach: small focused components, composed by consumers; almost every complex 'configurable' component is better as several primitives.

questions.explanation: Composition over configuration. Small focused components composed by consumers beats mega-buttons with many props.
